success_surface: Successfully implemented dynamic weighted revenue calculation using cross-sheet VLOOKUP formulas after iterative API debugging
effective_behaviors: Corrected openpyxl API parameter naming (col→column) on first error before proceeding
Inspected all available sheet names programmatically before accessing specific sheets
Examined both target worksheet structure and reference worksheet content before building formulas
Used dynamic lookup formulas (VLOOKUP) to map values between sheets rather than hardcoding
Applied formulas iteratively across entire data range rather than individual cells
Wrapped lookup formulas in IFERROR to handle potential missing matches gracefully
Verified formula placement and values after saving to confirm correct execution
Preserved original workbook by saving to designated output path
evidence: Fixed cell(row=i, col=j) → cell(row=row, column=column) parameter after TypeError on first attempt
Printed wb.sheetnames and ws.max_row/max_column before writing any formulas
Read Data sheet content showing stage→probability mapping before constructing VLOOKUP
Looped through range(start_row, last_row + 1) to apply formula to all data rows
Saved to specified output_path rather than modifying source file in-place
Loaded saved file back to verify formulas were written correctly

memory_items.title: API Parameter Correction Pattern
memory_items.lesson: When encountering TypeError about unexpected keyword arguments in openpyxl, check parameter name conventions (cell uses 'column=' not 'col=')
memory_items.generalization: Maintain awareness of library-specific API signatures; fix parameter naming on first error before proceeding with task

memory_items.title: Pre-Manipulation Structure Audit
memory_items.lesson: Before applying any changes, inspect sheet list, dimensions, and header row of both target and reference sheets
memory_items.generalization: Always gather structural metadata (sheetnames, max_row, max_column, headers) before implementing modifications

memory_items.title: Dynamic Cross-Sheet Formula Construction
memory_items.lesson: For calculations requiring lookup values from another sheet, construct relative formulas with absolute reference ranges (e.g., Data!$A:$B)
memory_items.generalization: Use lookup formulas (VLOOKUP/INDEX-MATCH) with mixed references to enable drag-fill behavior across rows while anchoring lookup table

memory_items.title: Range-Wise Formula Application
memory_items.lesson: Determine data bounds via max_row and iterate through all data rows to apply formulas uniformly
memory_items.generalization: Calculate target range from sheet dimensions rather than guessing; use loop over determined range

memory_items.title: Robust Formula Error Handling
memory_items.lesson: Wrap potentially-failing lookups in IFERROR to return safe defaults (like 0) when no match exists
memory_items.generalization: Include error mitigation in all formulas that perform external lookups or operations on variable data

edits.content:
## 6. Trace-Derived Lessons
- **API Parameter Vigilance**: Openpyxl's `cell()` method uses `column=` not `col=`; verify parameter names on first encounter to avoid TypeError
- **Bidirectional Sheet Inspection**: Examine both source data sheet (containing lookup values) and target sheet (receiving formulas) before construction
- **Relative-Absolute Reference Pattern**: Write lookup formulas with absolute lookup range ($A:$B) but relative row references (D2) to enable copy-paste efficiency across rows
- **Full-Range Determination**: Calculate `last_row = ws.max_row` and loop from start_row to last_row inclusive to ensure all data rows receive formulas
- **Formula Robustness**: Embed `IFERROR(lookup_formula, default_value)` around all cross-sheet lookups to handle missing entries gracefully
